`capture-session` naming convention from **file mtime** (`date -r "$src"`, which equals *last activity*
and drifts every turn until capture — it had produced a fabricated future `1953` stamp this session) to
the **first event timestamp in the `.jsonl`, converted to local time** — a stable anchor that makes the
name idempotent across re-captures and sorts the corpus by when each session *began*. Updated SKILL.md
§2 with the extraction snippet, and **back-filled all 18 prior pairs** via `git mv` (pure renames, no
content change). Two logs moved across a day boundary, correctly — `requirements-and-design-spec-creation`
(`05-30_0931`→`05-29_1845`) and `catalog-approval-and-scaffolding` (`05-30_0933`→`05-29_2146`) — because
they *started* on 05-29; the old mtime had mis-sorted them. **Why:** decided with the user after watching
the mtime drift live; a name that changes while you work can't identify a session.

## P8 — Version-resilience enhancements: CLI reference + changelog digest (post-v1, in progress)

**P8-requirements ✅ (2026-05-31)** — New requirement **R16** approved at the requirements gate: an
exhaustive, generated, version-resilient CLI reference. Added **additively** (R1–R15 untouched); it
anchors to R12 (version resilience), R13 (enforcement), R14 (dogfooding), R5/R6 (units U4/U10).
**Architecture decided with the user** — two artifacts in the committed-rendered pattern:
- **Tool 1** recursively introspects `claude --help` over subcommands into `meta/cli-reference.json` —
  the *authoritative machine truth* — unioned with a provenance-tracked
  `meta/cli-reference-supplement.yaml` (doc-only surface, e.g. in-REPL slash commands; provenance =
  doc URL + retrieval date). Each entry marks its source; nothing authored from memory (R12.AC3).
- **Tool 2** renders the learner-facing `course/reference/cli-reference.md` **from the machine source
  alone** (R16.AC2), drift-gated by a regenerate-and-diff freshness check in `make check` (R16.AC6).

On version drift (R12.AC6/AC7) **both** are regenerated from the installed CLI and the human page
re-rendered — bootstrapped by the drift trigger, not hand-maintained (R16.AC5).
**Why a machine intermediate** (raised with the user): mirrors the existing `version-data.yaml`→`.json`
twin pattern; one `--help` sweep feeds many consumers instead of re-shelling repeatedly; and it becomes
authoritative truth a *future* check can validate the curated `{{vd:key}}` flags against (drift the
names-only `cli-commands.snapshot` can't catch). **Dogfood (R16.AC7):** U10 (the spec-driven build is
the worked example) + U4 (the machine reference as single-source version data).
**P8-requirements-r17 ✅ (2026-05-31)** — Second requirement **R17** approved: a **CLI version-change
synopsis (changelog digest)** captured on every refresh. Kept a **standalone requirement** (not bolted
onto R16, not folded into R12 ACs) per the user's anti-shoehorn principle — it's the *narrative
changelog* artifact, distinct from R16's *exhaustive option reference* and R12's resilience spine, but

**L6 — awareness-tier depth** for coverage rows 27–29 may need more than a mention (row 10 / extended
thinking was handled as a U5 mention+pointer — P5-U5-vd — so it's no longer a concern).
_Resolve in:_ P5 (each home unit); home units assigned (P2-cov) — revisit only if a unit needs more depth.
_Also tracked in:_ coverage-matrix `tier_note`s.

**L7 — per-lab `start/`/`solution/` refs + verifiers not all created** (`start/uNN-labM` tag,
`solution/uNN-labM` branch).
_Resolve in:_ P5, per unit — each mutating lab gets its `start/` tag + a `SEEDED.md` §2 row +
`course/labs/<id>/verify.sh`. _Also tracked in:_ `codebases/SEEDED.md` §2; `tasks/P5-units.md`.
Status per lab (✅ = refs + verifier created, verified end-to-end fails-clean/passes-on-solution):
- **u01** ✅ (feature addition) · **u05** ✅ · **u06** ✅ (test-first overdue filter) · **u11** ✅
  (review lab: planted IDOR + wrong default + a false positive)
- **u07** ✅ (legacy D1 fix) · **u09** ✅ (legacy refactor, behavior-equivalence verifier) — legacy labs;
  both registered in `SEEDED.md` §2 as legacy entries (no *new* primary branch defect)
- **u02 / u03** — read-only, no `start/`/`solution/` refs (U2 prose answer key; U3 objective `verify.sh` + SEEDED row)
- **u08 / u10 / u12 / u13** — prose-self-check labs, no refs (decisions P5-U8-lab, P5-U10-lab, P5-U12-lab, P5-U13-lab)
- **u14** — no `start/`/`solution/` refs (hook in learner's `settings.json`), but self-check is an **objective pipe-test**, not prose (P5-U14-lab)
- **u15** — no `start/`/`solution/` refs (connection in learner's config + vetting decision), but self-check is objective: `claude mcp get` `✓ Connected` + verify tool result + vetting checklist (P5-U15-lab); ships verified `taskflow_mcp.py` + `taskflow.mcp.json` fixtures
- **u16** — no `start/`/`solution/` refs (headless run + worktrees + CI reading in learner's env), objective self-check on observables (`-p` output, `git worktree list` ≥2, per-diff review); CI dogfood is the existing `.github/workflows/checks.yml` (P5-U16-lab)
- **L7 status: all 16 P5 labs now accounted** — mutating labs (u01/u05/u06/u07/u09/u11) have `start/`+`solution/`+`verify.sh`; the rest are documented no-refs (read-only / prose-self-check / objective pipe-test). No P5 lab refs outstanding.

**~~L8~~ — ✅ CLOSED (P7, 2026-05-31).** Learner-prose ergonomics remediation complete. The post-v1
8-lens pass found the product mechanically/functionally clean; findings were confined to learner-facing
prose (M1 `R#` leakage; M2 bare `U#`/`C#`/`W#`/`CV` codes; L1 density; L2 under-declared
`reading_time_min` on U12–U14; L3 unexpanded `CV`) plus **M4** (raw `{{vd:key}}` tokens shown to
learners — an R15 miss) and **M5** (navigation). All resolved: the committed-rendered pattern
(`unit.src.md` → generated `unit.md` via `tools/render-units`) + the `tools/render-index` TOC closed
M4/M5, drift-gated in `make check`; **all 16 units** de-coded + migrated (7.6); capstone files +
`stuck.md` swept (7.7); convention docs updated for the split (7.8). L2 bumps applied (U12 8→10,
U13 8→12, U14 8→11). **Kept:** rubric `[Cn]`/`[CV]` tags, U10 teaching R-IDs, case-study `R1–R15`.
Committed in slices on `spec/quality-pass-phase`; `make check` green. _Tracked in:_
`tasks/P7-quality-pass.md`; decisions P7-render/P7-garble/P7-index/P7-prose/P7-rollout.
**Sole remaining (non-blocking) follow-up: T2** — optional unit-dir rename, deferred (P7-frontmatter/P7-T2).

**L9 (P8, 2026-05-31) — version-record drift-ahead.** `meta/cli-reference.json` is generated @ CLI
**2.1.159** while `meta/version-record.md` records the verified version as **2.1.158** — a deliberate,
documented gap (decision P8-no-bump: 2.1.159 is "internal infrastructure, no user-facing changes," so a
full re-verification bump is low-value churn now). `check-version-changelog` keys off the **recorded**
version, so `make check` stays green; `version-changelog.md` carries both the 2.1.158 baseline and a real
2.1.158 → 2.1.159 entry. _Resolve in:_ the next intentional refresh (re-verify the flagged keys against
the installed CLI, bump `version-record.md`, regenerate via `render-cli-reference --all`). _Also tracked
in:_ `meta/version-record.md`; decisions P8-no-bump, P8-execution.

**L10 (P8, 2026-05-31) — `cli-commands.snapshot` ⊂ `cli-reference.json`.** The older
`meta/cli-commands.snapshot` (names-only command list, consumed by `check-version-drift`) is now a strict
subset of the richer `cli-reference.json` (full command tree + flags + provenance). Kept separate for now
so `check-version-drift`'s lightweight command-list diff is unchanged. _Resolve in:_ a later cleanup —
fold the drift check onto `cli-reference.json` and retire the snapshot, or keep the snapshot as the cheap
fast-path and document the relationship. Non-blocking. _Also tracked in:_ `tasks/P8-cli-reference.md` §8.9.

**Decided, not open (do not re-litigate):** tools are no-extension kebab-case (deviation from design
§7 `.sh`, decision P3-tools); `permission-modes` value per verified CLI (P2-vd); awareness home-unit
assignments (P2-cov); primary substrate at ~1.65k LOC is accepted below the §7 "~2–4k" band (P4-loc);
`Comment` added as a 4th entity (P4-comment); the three legacy bugs D1–D3 stay baked into `legacy`
`main` — do **not** "fix" them (P4-bugs). These are settled calls recorded above in the per-phase sections.

**Sole remaining (non-blocking) follow-up: T2** — optional unit-dir rename, deferred (P7-frontmatter/P7-T2).

**L9 (P8, 2026-05-31) — version-record drift-ahead.** `meta/cli-reference.json` is generated @ CLI
**2.1.159** while `meta/version-record.md` records the verified version as **2.1.158** — a deliberate,
documented gap (decision P8-no-bump: 2.1.159 is "internal infrastructure, no user-facing changes," so a
full re-verification bump is low-value churn now). `check-version-changelog` keys off the **recorded**
version, so `make check` stays green; `version-changelog.md` carries both the 2.1.158 baseline and a real
2.1.158 → 2.1.159 entry. _Resolve in:_ the next intentional refresh (re-verify the flagged keys against
the installed CLI, bump `version-record.md`, regenerate via `render-cli-reference --all`). _Also tracked
in:_ `meta/version-record.md`; decisions P8-no-bump, P8-execution.

**L10 (P8, 2026-05-31) — `cli-commands.snapshot` ⊂ `cli-reference.json`.** The older
`meta/cli-commands.snapshot` (names-only command list, consumed by `check-version-drift`) is now a strict
subset of the richer `cli-reference.json` (full command tree + flags + provenance). Kept separate for now
so `check-version-drift`'s lightweight command-list diff is unchanged. _Resolve in:_ a later cleanup —
fold the drift check onto `cli-reference.json` and retire the snapshot, or keep the snapshot as the cheap
fast-path and document the relationship. Non-blocking. _Also tracked in:_ `tasks/P8-cli-reference.md` §8.9.

**Decided, not open (do not re-litigate):** tools are no-extension kebab-case (deviation from design
§7 `.sh`, decision P3-tools); `permission-modes` value per verified CLI (P2-vd); awareness home-unit
assignments (P2-cov); primary substrate at ~1.65k LOC is accepted below the §7 "~2–4k" band (P4-loc);
`Comment` added as a 4th entity (P4-comment); the three legacy bugs D1–D3 stay baked into `legacy`
`main` — do **not** "fix" them (P4-bugs). These are settled calls recorded above in the per-phase sections.

prose (M1 `R#` leakage; M2 bare `U#`/`C#`/`W#`/`CV` codes; L1 density; L2 under-declared

**2.1.159** while `meta/version-record.md` records the verified version as **2.1.158** — a deliberate,
documented gap (decision P8-no-bump: 2.1.159 is "internal infrastructure, no user-facing changes," so a
full re-verification bump is low-value churn now). `check-version-changelog` keys off the **recorded**
version, so `make check` stays green; `version-changelog.md` carries both the 2.1.158 baseline and a real
2.1.158 → 2.1.159 entry. _Resolve in:_ the next intentional refresh (re-verify the flagged keys against
the installed CLI, bump `version-record.md`, regenerate via `render-cli-reference --all`). _Also tracked
in:_ `meta/version-record.md`; decisions P8-no-bump, P8-execution.

subset of the richer `cli-reference.json` (full command tree + flags + provenance). Kept separate for now
so `check-version-drift`'s lightweight command-list diff is unchanged. _Resolve in:_ a later cleanup —

content.text:
I'm primed. Here's where things stand.

## Where we are
**P8 is COMPLETE** on branch `feat/cli-reference` — all 9 task slices (8.1–8.9) built and committed, `make check` green. P8 added the two post-v1 version-resilience enhancements:
- **R16** — generated, exhaustive CLI reference (`tools/render-cli-reference` → `meta/cli-reference.json` + supplement → `course/reference/cli-reference.md`)
- **R17** — CLI changelog digest (`meta/version-changelog.md` + `check-version-changelog`), with a learner-facing "What's new" section and inline `added_in` markers

Everything before it is done too: v1 build complete (P1–P6), the P7 quality pass complete, and the transcript-capture corpus back-filled.

## Open loops that bear on the next step
- **The one live action: P8 is not merged to `main`** — explicitly *awaiting your go-ahead* (CLAUDE.md ask-before-push). This is the obvious next move.
- **L9** — deliberate drift-ahead: `cli-reference.json` is generated @ CLI **2.1.159** but `version-record.md` records **2.1.158** (decision P8-no-bump — 2.1.159 is internal-only). Non-blocking; resolve at the next intentional refresh.
- **L10** — `cli-commands.snapshot` is now a subset of `cli-reference.json`; optional later cleanup. Non-blocking.
- **L1** — 2 version keys (`ci`, `managed-settings`) still `unverified`, blocked by access this env lacks (GitHub Actions / enterprise). Refresh debt, not a release blocker.
- **T2** — optional unit-dir rename, deferred.

## Working state
- Branch `feat/cli-reference`, **working tree clean**, up to date with origin. Nothing mid-flight.
- Latest commit is a capture-session log-naming refinement + corpus back-fill (7064c09).

## Next action
The natural next step is **merging `feat/cli-reference` → `main`** — it's the only thing P8 left outstanding, and it needs your explicit go-ahead before I touch `main` or push.

Want me to merge `feat/cli-reference` into `main` (and if so, push, or leave it local)? Or is there something else you'd like to pick up — e.g., the L10 snapshot cleanup, or a fresh CLI refresh to reconcile the L9 drift-ahead?

content.text:
The three `spec/*` branches are deleted. But **`quality-pass-p7` is not safe to delete** — and it's worth pausing on, because it's not what its name suggests.

It has 2 commits not in `main`:
- `ef3df43` — `P7 (WIP): roll learner-prose remediation through U2–U4; auto-de-code U5–U16`
- `7fe928c` — `Add quality-pass-session-1 log`

The diff against `main` shows it's a **stale, superseded** branch, not unfinished work you'd want back. It predates the canonical P7 rollout (which landed via `spec/quality-pass-phase`, now merged) and the whole P8 + transcript-corpus effort. Deleting it loses nothing real, but merging or restoring it would *regress* `main`: the diff would **remove** all of P8 (`cli-reference.json`, `render-cli-reference`, `version-changelog.md`, …), delete the renamed `.jsonl`/`.md` transcript corpus, and resurrect the old lossy `log/implementation-session-*.txt` files and the `unit.src.md` files that were intentionally cleaned up. Net: `33k insertions / 114k deletions` — it's the *old* world.

So it's genuinely abandoned WIP that was redone properly elsewhere. Two notes:
1. It's named `quality-pass-p7`, not `spec/*`, so it's outside your literal "merged spec branches" instruction.
2. It's **not merged**, so deleting needs the force flag (`-D`), which discards those 2 commits permanently (locally — they're not on the remote either, as far as I can see).

Given it's superseded, I'd recommend deleting it with `-D`. Want me to?

1	# Memory Index
2	
3	- [Project: Claude Code Mastery course](project-claude-code-mastery.md) — spec-driven training repo, novice→elite; P5 units in progress (U1–U12 done); live status in IMPLEMENTATION.md §3
4	- [Version-currency caveat](feedback-version-currency-caveat.md) — verify version-specific facts against installed CLI, not memory
5	- [Honor spec phase gates](feedback-spec-driven-honor-gates.md) — approve requirements before design, design before tasks
6	  - (Review-before-commit + ask-before-push are now repo working agreements in CLAUDE.md, so they apply for all users/machines.)
7	- [Project venv at .venv](feedback-uv-pip-install.md) — root `.venv/bin/python`; `make`/`PY` auto-detect it (no activation); create via `tools/doctor --setup-venv` (uv or pip)
8	- [Continuity hygiene](feedback-continuity-hygiene.md) — keep the 🔓 open-loops ledger + IMPLEMENTATION §3 current; user audits this

10	- [Small tool batches](feedback-small-tool-batches.md) — "console flakiness" was self-inflicted by oversized/duplicate batches; keep calls small, no dupes, no /tmp round-trips
11	- [Read tool over shell](feedback-read-tool-over-shell.md) — use Read (not sed/awk/grep/cat) to review file contents; shell grep only for locating across files

content.content:
1	---
2	name: project-claude-code-mastery
3	description: The claude-training repo is a spec-driven course to take devs from AI-novice to elite Claude Code user
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: 60da1c88-1049-436f-b19d-64dfecc96d3a
8	---
9	
10	`/home/jj/dev/claude-training` is a **spec-driven** training course: "Claude Code Mastery." Goal: take a seasoned developer new to AI coding → elite Claude Code practitioner covering all advanced features + industry best-practice workflows. **Latest-targeting, not pinned** — the verified CLI version lives in `meta/version-record.md` (do NOT hardcode a version from memory; see [[feedback-version-currency-caveat]]).
11	
12	Spec lives in `specs/claude-code-mastery/` using Kiro-style phases with approval gates: `requirements.md` (EARS) → `design.md` → `tasks.md`.
13	
14	**Locked decisions (2026-05-29):**
15	- Deliverable: Markdown curriculum + runnable hands-on labs against a single sample codebase + reference solutions (self-contained repo).
16	- F1 — **Outcome-driven**: use cases (jobs-to-be-done) are the primary organizing axis, prioritized by frequency × leverage; the 17 features are a *secondary coverage guarantee* (matrix, all in ≥1 example).
17	- F2 — **Tiered depth for senior engineers**: assume experienced fast learners; per-unit fast-path/TL;DR; no programming/Git/terminal basics; core deep, niche awareness-level.
18	- F3 — **Version-resilient, NOT pinned**: target the current/latest CLI; principles-first body with version-specific details quarantined as `{{vd:key}}` in `meta/version-data.yaml`; the spec is the update instrument with a documented refresh process.
19	- Assessment: **capstone only** — one graded project; units instructional, labs ungraded practice; rubric covers the full ladder.
20	- Audience: **self-paced solo only** — NO facilitator/cohort/instructor materials.
21	
22	**Key structural decisions from R-by-R review (2026-05-29):**
23	- R1 — progress tracked via **non-ranking capability map** of "can-do" statements grouped into neutral stages (First Wins → Daily Driver → Force Multiplier → Autonomy & Scale). NO novice/intermediate/elite labels in learner-facing text ("elite" is internal design target only).
24	- R7 — **two** sample codebases: primary + small deliberately-messy legacy repo (for onboarding/refactor/debug). Solutions as inspectable git artifacts.
25	- R10 — dedicated security unit + woven reinforcement; hands-on prompt-injection lab; verification defined as more than green tests.
26	- R12 — single machine-readable version-data file referenced by key; automated drift-detection; hard ban on memory-only version claims.
27	- R13 — required automated enforcement suite (front-matter validation, coverage/map cross-validation, traceability checks) run as local hooks + CI; doubles as worked examples.
28	- Resolved: Q3 (choose-your-own capstone brief + exemplar) and Q4 (macOS/Linux/WSL; native Windows out of v1). Still open for Design: Q1 (codebase stacks), Q2 (use-case catalog, capability map contents, course size).
29	
30	**Spec files in `specs/claude-code-mastery/`:** `requirements.md` (✅ APPROVED, R1–R15, frozen — changes need a recorded decision), `design.md` (✅ APPROVED & merged to `main`), `tasks.md` (chunked index → `tasks/P1–P6*.md` per-section files), `decisions.md` (rationale/why + rejected alternatives + the canonical 🔓 open-loops ledger; survives session boundary), `IMPLEMENTATION.md` (**START HERE** every session: read order, **§3 = live phase status**, context protocol, build Definition of Done), `README.md` (nav hub).
31	
32	**Resolved (no longer open):** Q1 domain = task/project tracker (`taskflow-api` primary + `taskflow-cli` legacy, both Python); Q2 = 16 content-driven units, capability map C1–C17 + CV. Q3/Q4 settled in session 1.
33	
34	**Live status is `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3 — trust it over this file.** As of 2026-05-30: P1–P4 ✅ done; **P5 ✅ COMPLETE — all 16 units (U1–U16) authored; every can-do C1–C17+CV practiced by ≥1 lab**; **P6 next** (capstone/case-study/finalization — also clears L3 `check-strict`). U12 built the repo's first real `.claude/` artifacts as dogfooded worked examples, both genuinely used by the operator: **`/close-unit <NN>`** (command — runs the per-unit state-sync chore: IMPLEMENTATION §3 + tasks box/bullet + decisions/ledger + version-currency + `make check`) and **`prime-context`** (skill — loads full project context at session start). Practical: run `/prime-context` when resuming a session; run `/close-unit NN` when a unit's prose is done ([[feedback-continuity-hygiene]]). Earlier `new-unit`/`unit-check` candidates were swapped out as props (no real consumer). Work one unit per slice (context protocol); `make check` is the authoring gate (stays green), `make check-strict` is the v1-done gate (red until P6). Branch `spec/tasks-phase`; remote `origin` (gitea) — [[feedback-ask-before-push]]. Honor phase gates ([[feedback-spec-driven-honor-gates]]), keep §3 + the ledger current ([[feedback-continuity-hygiene]]), verify version-specifics vs installed CLI ([[feedback-version-currency-caveat]]).
35

---
name: project-claude-code-mastery
description: The claude-training repo is a spec-driven course to take devs from AI-novice to elite Claude Code user
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 60da1c88-1049-436f-b19d-64dfecc96d3a
---

`/home/jj/dev/claude-training` is a **spec-driven** training course: "Claude Code Mastery." Goal: take a seasoned developer new to AI coding → elite Claude Code practitioner covering all advanced features + industry best-practice workflows. **Latest-targeting, not pinned** — the verified CLI version lives in `meta/version-record.md` (do NOT hardcode a version from memory; see [[feedback-version-currency-caveat]]).

Spec lives in `specs/claude-code-mastery/` using Kiro-style phases with approval gates: `requirements.md` (EARS) → `design.md` → `tasks.md`.

**Locked decisions (2026-05-29):**
- Deliverable: Markdown curriculum + runnable hands-on labs against a single sample codebase + reference solutions (self-contained repo).
- F1 — **Outcome-driven**: use cases (jobs-to-be-done) are the primary organizing axis, prioritized by frequency × leverage; the 17 features are a *secondary coverage guarantee* (matrix, all in ≥1 example).
- F2 — **Tiered depth for senior engineers**: assume experienced fast learners; per-unit fast-path/TL;DR; no programming/Git/terminal basics; core deep, niche awareness-level.
- F3 — **Version-resilient, NOT pinned**: target the current/latest CLI; principles-first body with version-specific details quarantined as `{{vd:key}}` in `meta/version-data.yaml`; the spec is the update instrument with a documented refresh process.
- Assessment: **capstone only** — one graded project; units instructional, labs ungraded practice; rubric covers the full ladder.
- Audience: **self-paced solo only** — NO facilitator/cohort/instructor materials.

**Key structural decisions from R-by-R review (2026-05-29):**
- R1 — progress tracked via **non-ranking capability map** of "can-do" statements grouped into neutral stages (First Wins → Daily Driver → Force Multiplier → Autonomy & Scale). NO novice/intermediate/elite labels in learner-facing text ("elite" is internal design target only).
- R7 — **two** sample codebases: primary + small deliberately-messy legacy repo (for onboarding/refactor/debug). Solutions as inspectable git artifacts.
- R10 — dedicated security unit + woven reinforcement; hands-on prompt-injection lab; verification defined as more than green tests.
- R12 — single machine-readable version-data file referenced by key; automated drift-detection; hard ban on memory-only version claims.
- R13 — required automated enforcement suite (front-matter validation, coverage/map cross-validation, traceability checks) run as local hooks + CI; doubles as worked examples.
- Resolved: Q3 (choose-your-own capstone brief + exemplar) and Q4 (macOS/Linux/WSL; native Windows out of v1). Still open for Design: Q1 (codebase stacks), Q2 (use-case catalog, capability map contents, course size).

**Spec files in `specs/claude-code-mastery/`:** `requirements.md` (✅ APPROVED, R1–R15, frozen — changes need a recorded decision), `design.md` (✅ APPROVED & merged to `main`), `tasks.md` (chunked index → `tasks/P1–P6*.md` per-section files), `decisions.md` (rationale/why + rejected alternatives + the canonical 🔓 open-loops ledger; survives session boundary), `IMPLEMENTATION.md` (**START HERE** every session: read order, **§3 = live phase status**, context protocol, build Definition of Done), `README.md` (nav hub).

**Resolved (no longer open):** Q1 domain = task/project tracker (`taskflow-api` primary + `taskflow-cli` legacy, both Python); Q2 = 16 content-driven units, capability map C1–C17 + CV. Q3/Q4 settled in session 1.

**Live status is `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3 — trust it over this file.** As of 2026-05-30: P1–P4 ✅ done; **P5 ✅ COMPLETE — all 16 units (U1–U16) authored; every can-do C1–C17+CV practiced by ≥1 lab**; **P6 next** (capstone/case-study/finalization — also clears L3 `check-strict`). U12 built the repo's first real `.claude/` artifacts as dogfooded worked examples, both genuinely used by the operator: **`/close-unit <NN>`** (command — runs the per-unit state-sync chore: IMPLEMENTATION §3 + tasks box/bullet + decisions/ledger + version-currency + `make check`) and **`prime-context`** (skill — loads full project context at session start). Practical: run `/prime-context` when resuming a session; run `/close-unit NN` when a unit's prose is done ([[feedback-continuity-hygiene]]). Earlier `new-unit`/`unit-check` candidates were swapped out as props (no real consumer). Work one unit per slice (context protocol); `make check` is the authoring gate (stays green), `make check-strict` is the v1-done gate (red until P6). Branch `spec/tasks-phase`; remote `origin` (gitea) — [[feedback-ask-before-push]]. Honor phase gates ([[feedback-spec-driven-honor-gates]]), keep §3 + the ledger current ([[feedback-continuity-hygiene]]), verify version-specifics vs installed CLI ([[feedback-version-currency-caveat]]).

content.content:
1	---
2	name: feedback-version-currency-caveat
3	description: "For the claude-training course, never trust model memory for version-specific Claude Code facts"
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: feedback
7	  originSessionId: 60da1c88-1049-436f-b19d-64dfecc96d3a
8	---
9	
10	The course is **version-resilient, not pinned** (decision F3): it targets the current/latest Claude Code CLI, NOT a fixed version. When authoring [[project-claude-code-mastery]], every version-specific detail (commands, flags, file paths, settings keys, feature availability) must be verified against the **actually-installed** latest CLI — via `--help`, `/help`, official docs — not from model memory.
11	
12	**Why:** The assistant's training cutoff (Aug 2025) predates current CLI releases, so memory-based feature claims risk being outdated or hallucinated. Version resilience + the update process is requirement R12.
13	
14	**How to apply:** Write durable concepts in the body; quarantine version-specific details into marked callouts + a single version record/matrix. Verify before writing; mark unverifiable claims. Record the verified version + date (R12.AC4) and follow the documented refresh process (R12.AC5) on each update.
15

When authoring [[project-claude-code-mastery]], every version-specific detail (commands, flags, file paths, settings keys, feature availability) must be verified against the **actually-installed** latest CLI — via `--help`, `/help`, official docs — not from model memory.

**Why:** The assistant's training cutoff (Aug 2025) predates current CLI releases, so memory-based feature claims risk being outdated or hallucinated. Version resilience + the update process is requirement R12.

**How to apply:** Write durable concepts in the body; quarantine version-specific details into marked callouts + a single version record/matrix. Verify before writing; mark unverifiable claims. Record the verified version + date (R12.AC4) and follow the documented refresh process (R12.AC5) on each update.

1	---
2	name: feedback-spec-driven-honor-gates
3	description: "On the claude-training spec, honor phase-approval gates — don't write design before requirements are approved"
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: feedback
7	  originSessionId: 60da1c88-1049-436f-b19d-64dfecc96d3a
8	---
9	
10	For [[project-claude-code-mastery]], follow spec-driven discipline strictly: complete and get approval on `requirements.md` before writing `design.md`, and approve `design.md` before generating `tasks.md`.
11	
12	**Why:** The whole point (and a teaching example, R3.AC2) is that design built on unreviewed requirements is waste. The user wants requirements + design done in one session, but "one session" still respects the gate — request approval between phases.
13	
14	**How to apply:** After drafting a phase, stop and ask for sign-off/edits; only then proceed. Keep requirement IDs (R1–R13) stable for traceability across files.
15

content.content:
1	---
2	name: feedback-uv-pip-install
3	description: "The project uses a root .venv (.venv/bin/python); make/PY auto-detect it, no activation needed; create it via tools/doctor --setup-venv (uv or pip)"
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: feedback
7	  originSessionId: 7b80c621-d0b2-4c18-8155-cae8bb3e270c
8	---
9	
10	The project's Python virtualenv lives at the **repo-root `.venv`** (the location CLAUDE.md and the
11	README document). Run Python through **`make`** (its `PY` auto-detects `.venv/bin/python` when
12	present) or call **`.venv/bin/python`** directly — **no activation step is needed**, and don't rely
13	on a manually-activated venv (a forgotten activation caused an `exit 127` cascade on 2026-05-31).
14	Never run a bare `python3 tools/...` — without `.venv` on PATH it hits system Python, which lacks the
15	deps.
16	
17	**Creating it (now expected, not forbidden):** `tools/doctor --setup-venv` (or `make venv`) creates
18	`.venv` and installs `pyyaml` + `jsonschema`, preferring `uv` and falling back to `pip`
19	(`--pip` forces pip). A plain `tools/doctor` run with missing deps prints the same uv/pip
20	instructions. To add a package later, `uv pip install --python .venv/bin/python <pkg>`.
21	
22	**Why:** on 2026-05-31 the user decided to standardize on a project-root `.venv` per the repo docs and
23	have `doctor` bootstrap it for learners — superseding the earlier "a venv is already active at
24	`/home/jj/venvs/main`, never create one" guidance (that path is deprecated; `make`/`doctor` no longer
25	depend on it). See [[project-claude-code-mastery]].
26

file.content:
---
name: feedback-uv-pip-install
description: "The project uses a root .venv (.venv/bin/python); make/PY auto-detect it, no activation needed; create it via tools/doctor --setup-venv (uv or pip)"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: feedback
  originSessionId: 7b80c621-d0b2-4c18-8155-cae8bb3e270c
---

The project's Python virtualenv lives at the **repo-root `.venv`** (the location CLAUDE.md and the
README document). Run Python through **`make`** (its `PY` auto-detects `.venv/bin/python` when
present) or call **`.venv/bin/python`** directly — **no activation step is needed**, and don't rely
on a manually-activated venv (a forgotten activation caused an `exit 127` cascade on 2026-05-31).
Never run a bare `python3 tools/...` — without `.venv` on PATH it hits system Python, which lacks the
deps.

**Creating it (now expected, not forbidden):** `tools/doctor --setup-venv` (or `make venv`) creates
`.venv` and installs `pyyaml` + `jsonschema`, preferring `uv` and falling back to `pip`
(`--pip` forces pip). A plain `tools/doctor` run with missing deps prints the same uv/pip
instructions. To add a package later, `uv pip install --python .venv/bin/python <pkg>`.

**Why:** on 2026-05-31 the user decided to standardize on a project-root `.venv` per the repo docs and
have `doctor` bootstrap it for learners — superseding the earlier "a venv is already active at
`/home/jj/venvs/main`, never create one" guidance (that path is deprecated; `make`/`doctor` no longer
depend on it). See [[project-claude-code-mastery]].

1	---
2	name: feedback-continuity-hygiene
3	description: User audits session-to-session continuity; keep the open-loops ledger and IMPLEMENTATION §3 current
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: feedback
7	  originSessionId: 7b80c621-d0b2-4c18-8155-cae8bb3e270c
8	---
9	
10	For the claude-training spec build, the user proactively checks whether deferrals and deviations
11	are being tracked for future sessions (he asked "are we effectively tracking diversions and
12	deferrals?" unprompted). Treat continuity hygiene as a first-class deliverable, not an afterthought.
13	
14	**Why:** The build deliberately spans many sessions/context windows. Scattered or stale tracking
15	silently misleads the next session — the user caught a stale orientation doc and had me consolidate
16	the deferrals into one ledger.
17	
18	**How to apply:**
19	- The canonical open-loops ledger is `specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md` → "Open loops &
20	  deferrals 🔓". When you defer/divert anything, add a row immediately; strike it through (with the
21	  closing phase/commit) when resolved.
22	- Refresh `specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3 "Current state" and the `tasks.md` status
23	  header at **every phase boundary** — §3 is the first file a fresh session reads.
24	- Don't let parallel lists (e.g. `meta/version-record.md` "Outstanding to verify") drift; point them
25	  into the ledger.
26	
27	See [[project-claude-code-mastery]] and [[feedback-spec-driven-honor-gates]].
28

content.content:
1	---
2	name: feedback-ask-before-push
3	description: Always ask for confirmation before pushing to the git remote
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: feedback
7	  originSessionId: e841cbb1-089b-4114-8712-31998d589e19
8	---
9	
10	Always ask the user before pushing to the git remote (`git push`), every time — even when
11	mid-task and the push seems like the obvious next step.
12	
13	**Why:** The user said explicitly (2026-05-30): "Ensure you always ask before pushing to the
14	remote in the future." Pushing is outward-facing and publishes work to a shared remote
15	(`origin` = gitea.marlow.home:marlow/claude-training).
16	
17	**How to apply:** Local commits, tags, and branches are fine to create as part of the work
18	(see [[feedback-continuity-hygiene]] and the spec's lab-ref workflow). But stop and ask before
19	any `git push` — branch, tag, or solution branch. One explicit "push now" authorizes only that
20	push, not future ones.
21

push, not future ones.

1	---
2	name: feedback-small-tool-batches
3	description: "Console \"flakiness\" was self-inflicted by oversized/duplicate tool batches — keep batches small, no duplicates, prefer dedicated tools"
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: feedback
7	  originSessionId: a2724d32-ece9-4efe-a3e3-0736c86604dd
8	---
9	
10	Apparent "console flakiness" (duplicated output, dropped output, phantom lines like a stray
11	`## done (non-strict)` inside a file) is **not** an environment bug. A clean diagnostic proved
12	single, small tool calls return faithful output every time (`printf`, `Read`, `tty`, multi-file
13	`grep` all clean and exactly once).
14	
15	**Why:** the garbling came from firing huge single-message batches (10–15 tool calls), many of them
16	**exact duplicates** — the harness flagged this repeatedly with `Wasted call — file unchanged since
17	your last Read`. When many results return interleaved, output from one call bleeds into how another
18	is read, so it *looks* like corruption/duplication. The `/tmp`-redirect-then-`Read` "workaround"
19	only added more calls and noise; it fixed nothing.
20	
21	**How to apply:**
22	- One call per distinct need — never re-issue the same Read/Bash in a batch.
23	- Keep batches small and homogeneous; don't mix a dozen Reads + Bash calls in one turn.
24	- Use `Read` to read files (not `cat`/`sed`/`head`); use dedicated search/file tools over shell
25	  pipelines (`grep`/`awk`/`sed`/`echo`) — this matches the standing system-prompt guidance.
26	- Reserve `Bash` for real actions (git, make, pytest, chmod) and simple one-shot queries.
27	- Do **not** round-trip output through `/tmp`.
28	- **Never batch a write and its dependent read together.** Results in a parallel batch all return
29	  at the *end*, so a `Read` of a file a same-batch `Bash` is still writing races and reads stale/empty
30	  content — exactly the system-prompt rule "if calls have dependencies you MUST wait." Empty output
31	  mid-batch means "not finished," not "failed" — don't pile on duplicate calls to compensate.
32	  (Resurfaced worse on 2026-05-31: a `… > /tmp/x` + `Read /tmp/x` race, compounded by a stale venv
33	  path that made the write fail with exit 127. The user flagged it as a consistent regression.)
34	- **Before recording any "I found/changed/fixed X" claim, confirm it against a clean ground-truth
35	  read.** Phantom reads still slip through occasionally: in U4 a phantom showed a malformed
36	  `settings.json` (stray brace) that was actually valid — `json.load` even said so — and the false
37	  "fixed it" claim got propagated to four files before a clean re-read caught it. Trust the
38	  validator/ground-truth over a remembered read; never act on a single suspicious read.
39	
40	Confirmed with the user (2026-05-30) after a controlled test. Relates to [[feedback-continuity-hygiene]].
41

file.content:
---
name: feedback-small-tool-batches
description: "Console \"flakiness\" was self-inflicted by oversized/duplicate tool batches — keep batches small, no duplicates, prefer dedicated tools"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: feedback
  originSessionId: a2724d32-ece9-4efe-a3e3-0736c86604dd
---

Apparent "console flakiness" (duplicated output, dropped output, phantom lines like a stray
`## done (non-strict)` inside a file) is **not** an environment bug. A clean diagnostic proved
single, small tool calls return faithful output every time (`printf`, `Read`, `tty`, multi-file
`grep` all clean and exactly once).

**Why:** the garbling came from firing huge single-message batches (10–15 tool calls), many of them
**exact duplicates** — the harness flagged this repeatedly with `Wasted call — file unchanged since
your last Read`. When many results return interleaved, output from one call bleeds into how another
is read, so it *looks* like corruption/duplication. The `/tmp`-redirect-then-`Read` "workaround"
only added more calls and noise; it fixed nothing.

**How to apply:**
- One call per distinct need — never re-issue the same Read/Bash in a batch.
- Keep batches small and homogeneous; don't mix a dozen Reads + Bash calls in one turn.
- Use `Read` to read files (not `cat`/`sed`/`head`); use dedicated search/file tools over shell
  pipelines (`grep`/`awk`/`sed`/`echo`) — this matches the standing system-prompt guidance.
- Reserve `Bash` for real actions (git, make, pytest, chmod) and simple one-shot queries.
- Do **not** round-trip output through `/tmp`.
- **Never batch a write and its dependent read together.** Results in a parallel batch all return
  at the *end*, so a `Read` of a file a same-batch `Bash` is still writing races and reads stale/empty
  content — exactly the system-prompt rule "if calls have dependencies you MUST wait." Empty output
  mid-batch means "not finished," not "failed" — don't pile on duplicate calls to compensate.
  (Resurfaced worse on 2026-05-31: a `… > /tmp/x` + `Read /tmp/x` race, compounded by a stale venv
  path that made the write fail with exit 127. The user flagged it as a consistent regression.)
- **Before recording any "I found/changed/fixed X" claim, confirm it against a clean ground-truth
  read.** Phantom reads still slip through occasionally: in U4 a phantom showed a malformed
  `settings.json` (stray brace) that was actually valid — `json.load` even said so — and the false
  "fixed it" claim got propagated to four files before a clean re-read caught it. Trust the
  validator/ground-truth over a remembered read; never act on a single suspicious read.

Confirmed with the user (2026-05-30) after a controlled test. Relates to [[feedback-continuity-hygiene]].

3	description: Use the Read tool (not sed/awk/grep/cat) to review file contents
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: feedback
7	  originSessionId: e8c2a669-1cfc-48f5-8981-dff3db943624
8	---
9	
10	When reviewing or inspecting file contents, use the dedicated **Read** tool, not shell
11	(`sed`/`awk`/`grep`/`cat`). The user corrected this twice in one session.
12	
13	**Why:** it's the intended tool and gives a better display; Edit/Write also require a prior
14	Read of the file, so reaching for shell just means re-reading later. Shell text-munging on
15	files you intend to edit is wasted work.
16	
17	**How to apply:** default to Read for file contents. Reserve shell `grep`/`rg` for *locating*
18	matches across many files (a search), not for reading a known file's contents. Relates to
19	[[feedback-small-tool-batches]].
20

metadata:

---

When reviewing or inspecting file contents, use the dedicated **Read** tool, not shell
(`sed`/`awk`/`grep`/`cat`). The user corrected this twice in one session.

**Why:** it's the intended tool and gives a better display; Edit/Write also require a prior
Read of the file, so reaching for shell just means re-reading later. Shell text-munging on
files you intend to edit is wasted work.

**How to apply:** default to Read for file contents. Reserve shell `grep`/`rg` for *locating*
matches across many files (a search), not for reading a known file's contents. Relates to
[[feedback-small-tool-batches]].

aiTitle: Switch to main and pull latest changes

content.text:
Note to self: I duplicated that batch — exactly the [[feedback-small-tool-batches]] mistake. The first set returned fine; the dupes wasted calls. Moving on.

Here's my audit. I read all 8 memory files plus the index against current project reality (v1 + P7 + P8 all complete and merged to `main`).

## Stale (factually wrong now)

**1. `MEMORY.md` index, line 3** — says *"P5 units in progress (U1–U12 done)"*. Reality: all 16 units done, P6/P7/P8 complete, merged to main. Wrong by three phases.

**2. `project-claude-code-mastery.md`, the "Live status" paragraph (line 34)** — the worst offender. Says *"As of 2026-05-30: P5 ✅ … P6 next"*, references branch `spec/tasks-phase` (deleted today). This paragraph also **violates the memory rule against duplicating live status** that already lives in `IMPLEMENTATION.md §3`. It should be a pointer, not a snapshot. (Lines 28/32 also carry contradictory "still open / resolved" Q1–Q4 framing.)

## Possibly redundant (your call)

**3. `feedback-ask-before-push.md`** — ask-before-push is now a **CLAUDE.md working agreement** (the index itself notes this on line 6). Memory guidance says don't store what CLAUDE.md already records. It's a candidate for deletion — though it does preserve the explicit user quote + rationale.

## Healthy — keep as-is
- `feedback-version-currency-caveat` — durable, still governs refreshes
- `feedback-continuity-hygiene` — durable
- `feedback-small-tool-batches` — durable (and I just proved it)
- `feedback-read-tool-over-shell` — durable
- `feedback-uv-pip-install` — current (updated today)
- `feedback-spec-driven-honor-gates` — durable principle (one nit: says "R1–R13", now R1–R17)

My plan: **fix #1 and #2** (clear staleness — update index, trim the project file's status to a pointer + correct the branch/Q-state). For **#3**, I lean toward deleting it since CLAUDE.md covers it for all machines. Let me confirm scope before editing.

options.description: Update the two stale spots but keep ask-before-push as belt-and-suspenders reinforcement of the CLAUDE.md rule.

options.label: Aggressive trim
options.description: Fix stale, delete ask-before-push, AND fold the project file's locked-decisions down hard (it largely restates the spec/decisions.md — keep only what's non-derivable).

For [[project-claude-code-mastery]], follow spec-driven discipline strictly: complete and get approval on `requirements.md` before writing `design.md`, and approve `design.md` before generating `tasks.md`.

message.content: I need a strategy to keep the claude development environment uniform across different machines. Local machine memory makes that very difficult. I am considering starting a rule of only running claude code without memory enabled, and move everyth relavent to this project in memory now to the CLAUDE.md or other appropriate files as best practice dictates. Is that a common pattern?

content.text:
Good question, and it's worth answering as strategy before touching anything. Short version: **yes — version-controlled files are the standard mechanism for uniformity, and for a project whose whole thesis is "source of truth is the files, not memory," disabling auto-memory is the most consistent choice you could make.** But "put everything in CLAUDE.md" is slightly too blunt; the right model sorts facts by *audience*, and one category doesn't have a natural home in any single repo.

## Why your instinct is right

The layers differ by what travels:

| Layer | Scope | Travels across machines? |
|---|---|---|
| `CLAUDE.md`, `meta/`, `.claude/{commands,skills,agents,settings.json}`, hooks | Project | **Yes — via git** |
| Auto-memory (`~/.claude/projects/.../memory/`) | Project, **per-machine** | **No** ← your problem |
| `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (user-global), `settings.local.json` | You / this machine | No (unless *you* sync it) |

The uniformity mechanism isn't CLAUDE.md *per se* — it's **git**. Auto-memory is precisely the layer that doesn't get committed, so on N machines it silently diverges. That's the friction you're feeling. Checked-in files are also reviewable (they show up in diffs), which memory never is.

This is an established pattern: Anthropic positions `CLAUDE.md` + the `.claude/` directory as the shareable/team layer, and auto-memory as a newer per-user convenience. Teams and multi-machine setups lean on the committed files. So you're not inventing anything.

## The nuance: sort by audience, not all-into-CLAUDE.md

Three buckets:

- **A — Project facts that must be uniform for anyone in this repo** → `CLAUDE.md` / `meta/` (committed). Wrong to leave in memory.
- **B — *Your* working-style preferences that span all your projects** → user-global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`. Putting these in *this* repo's CLAUDE.md is wrong altitude — they'd only apply when you're here, and they'd leak your personal prefs to collaborators.
- **C — Genuinely machine-local/ephemeral** → memory. You currently have **none** of this.

Mapping your current memory:

| Memory | Bucket | Where it goes | Already covered? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `project-claude-code-mastery` | A | CLAUDE.md + IMPLEMENTATION.md | **Yes — fully redundant** |
| `version-currency-caveat` | A | CLAUDE.md working agreement | **Yes** ("never author a version value from memory") |
| `uv-pip-install` (venv) | A | CLAUDE.md "Stack" | **Yes** |
| `continuity-hygiene` | A | CLAUDE.md ("Keep state current") | Mostly |
| `spec-driven-honor-gates` | A | CLAUDE.md / IMPLEMENTATION.md | Mostly |
| `small-tool-batches` | **B** | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` | No — and it's *not* project-specific |
| `read-tool-over-shell` | **B** | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` | No — generic |

So bucket A is ~already in CLAUDE.md; the only real migration is the two bucket-B items.

## The one gap to plan around

CLAUDE.md alone **doesn't** give you cross-project uniformity for bucket B, because `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is itself machine-local. The complete answer is: **a dotfiles repo you clone on each machine** that carries `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (and optionally global commands/skills/settings). Then:

- Project knowledge → committed in each repo.
- Personal cross-project knowledge → committed in dotfiles.
- Machine-local → the small remainder, which you've decided to drop.

That fully closes the loop, memory off, nothing lost.

## Recommendation

Do it — it's aligned with this repo's own teaching (CLAUDE.md working agreement #1 is literally *"Source of truth is the files, not memory"*, and U4 teaches this). Concretely:

1. Confirm bucket A is captured in `CLAUDE.md` (tighten the 1–2 "mostly" rows).
2. Move `small-tool-batches` + `read-tool-over-shell` into `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (then sync that via dotfiles).
3. Empty the project memory dir.
4. Turn off auto-memory.

One honesty caveat consistent with your own version-currency rule: the **exact setting to disable auto-memory** is a CLI-version detail I won't assert from memory — I'd verify it against your installed CLI/`/help` (or the `claude-code-guide` agent) before writing it into settings. Steps 1–3 I can do right now regardless.

Want me to (a) verify the disable mechanism first, (b) start the migration of the two bucket-B items + the CLAUDE.md tightening, or both?

message.content: Lets execute both. Be careful not to batch race conditions again.

input.prompt:
I need to know how to DISABLE the auto-memory / persistent file-based memory feature in Claude Code (the feature that writes per-project memory files under ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/ and auto-loads them into context each session). 

Specifically:
1. What is the exact setting in settings.json (key name, valid values, and which settings file it belongs in) that turns this auto-memory feature OFF?
2. Is there an environment variable equivalent?
3. Verify against the actually installed CLI version on this machine (run `claude --help`, check docs, or whatever authoritative source you can) — do NOT answer from training memory alone, because this is a version-specific detail and the setting may be recent.

Please report the exact, verified key/value and its provenance (where you confirmed it). If you cannot verify it against the installed CLI or official docs, say so explicitly rather than guessing.

content.text:
Perfect! I have the authoritative information from the official Claude Code documentation. Here's your answer:

## Auto Memory Disable Setting — Verified Against Official Docs

### 1. **settings.json Key**

To disable auto memory, use this setting in any scope (user, project, or local):

```json
{
  "autoMemoryEnabled": false
}
```

**Location options:**
- `~/.claude/settings.json` (user scope — affects all projects)
- `.claude/settings.json` (project scope — affects this repo)
- `.claude/settings.local.json` (local scope — personal overrides, gitignored)

### 2. **Environment Variable**

```bash
CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY=1
```

### 3. **In-Session Toggle**

During an interactive Claude Code session, you can also run:
```
/memory
```

This opens the memory browser where you can toggle auto memory on or off for the current session.

---

## Provenance

This is from the official **Claude Code documentation** at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory.md (section "Enable or disable auto memory") and https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings.md (section "Auto Memory Setting"). Both confirm:

- **Key:** `autoMemoryEnabled` with boolean values `true` or `false`
- **Environment variable:** `CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY=1`
- **Requirement:** Claude Code v2.1.59 or later

The setting works at any [settings scope](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings#configuration-files) with proper [scope priority](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/settings#scope-priority-example) (managed > local > project > user).

input.content:
# Personal working agreements (jj)

These are my cross-project working-style preferences. They apply on every machine I clone my
dotfiles to; project-specific facts belong in each repo's own `CLAUDE.md`, not here.

## Tool use: small, faithful batches

Apparent "console flakiness" (duplicated/dropped output, phantom lines) is almost always
self-inflicted by oversized or duplicate tool batches — not an environment bug. A controlled test
proved single, small calls return faithful output every time.

- One call per distinct need — never re-issue the same Read/Bash in a batch (the harness flags it
  `Wasted call — file unchanged since your last Read`).
- Keep batches small and homogeneous; don't fire a dozen mixed Reads + Bash calls at once.
- **Never batch a write and its dependent read together.** Parallel results all return at the end,
  so a Read of a file a same-batch Bash is still writing races and reads stale/empty content. Empty
  output mid-batch means "not finished," not "failed" — don't pile on duplicate calls to compensate.
- Don't round-trip output through `/tmp`; it only adds calls and noise.
- Before recording any "I found/changed/fixed X" claim, confirm it against a clean ground-truth read
  or a validator. Phantom reads occasionally slip through — trust the validator over a remembered read.

## File contents: Read, not shell

Use the **Read** tool to review or inspect a file's contents — not `cat`/`sed`/`awk`/`head`. Edit and
Write require a prior Read anyway, so reaching for shell text-munging on a file I intend to edit is
wasted work. Reserve shell `grep`/`rg` for *locating* matches across many files (a search), not for
reading a known file.

# Personal working agreements (jj)

These are my cross-project working-style preferences. They apply on every machine I clone my
dotfiles to; project-specific facts belong in each repo's own `CLAUDE.md`, not here.

## Tool use: small, faithful batches

Apparent "console flakiness" (duplicated/dropped output, phantom lines) is almost always
self-inflicted by oversized or duplicate tool batches — not an environment bug. A controlled test
proved single, small calls return faithful output every time.

- One call per distinct need — never re-issue the same Read/Bash in a batch (the harness flags it
  `Wasted call — file unchanged since your last Read`).
- Keep batches small and homogeneous; don't fire a dozen mixed Reads + Bash calls at once.
- **Never batch a write and its dependent read together.** Parallel results all return at the end,
  so a Read of a file a same-batch Bash is still writing races and reads stale/empty content. Empty
  output mid-batch means "not finished," not "failed" — don't pile on duplicate calls to compensate.
- Don't round-trip output through `/tmp`; it only adds calls and noise.
- Before recording any "I found/changed/fixed X" claim, confirm it against a clean ground-truth read
  or a validator. Phantom reads occasionally slip through — trust the validator over a remembered read.

## File contents: Read, not shell

Use the **Read** tool to review or inspect a file's contents — not `cat`/`sed`/`awk`/`head`. Edit and
Write require a prior Read anyway, so reaching for shell text-munging on a file I intend to edit is
wasted work. Reserve shell `grep`/`rg` for *locating* matches across many files (a search), not for
reading a known file.

# CLAUDE.md — project memory for the Claude Code Mastery course

This file is **authentic dogfooding**: it is the real project memory used to build and maintain
this course, and unit **U4 (Memory & context)** references it as a living example (R14.AC1). Keep
it accurate — `tools/check-links` / `tools/check-version-refs` guard the references that point here.

## What this repo is

A self-paced, solo, hands-on **course** that takes a seasoned developer who is new to AI-assisted
coding to elite Claude Code practitioner. It is **outcome-driven** (organized around real use cases,
not a feature tour), **tiered** for fast-learning senior engineers, and **version-resilient**
(targets the latest CLI; refreshed via its own spec). The course is built **with** Claude Code,
spec-driven — so the repo is itself a worked example of what it teaches.

## How this repo is organized

- `specs/claude-code-mastery/` — the spec (requirements → design → tasks, + decisions, IMPLEMENTATION).
  **Read `specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md` first** in any fresh session.
- `meta/` — single-source machine-readable artifacts (capability map, use-case catalog, coverage
  matrix, version data, workflows, glossary, conventions, unit schema + templates). Referenced by
  key, never duplicated.
- `course/` — units, capstone, progress checklist, stuck guide, maintainer guide.
- `codebases/primary/` (`taskflow-api`) and `codebases/legacy/` (`taskflow-cli`) — lab substrates.
- `tools/` — doctor, enforcement checks, drift detection, verify/reset-lab.
- `.claude/` — dogfooded commands, skills, hooks, settings. `.github/workflows/` — CI backstop.

## Working agreements (for any session editing this repo)

- **Source of truth is the files, not memory.** Trust `meta/*` and the spec over recollection.
- **Never author a version-specific value from memory.** Put it in `meta/version-data.yaml` with
  provenance, verified against the installed CLI, and reference it as `{{vd:key}}` (R12.AC3). The
  current verified CLI is recorded in `meta/version-record.md`.
- **Cross-cutting facts live once.** Add to the relevant `meta/` file and reference it; don't inline.
- **Conventions** (naming, layout, tags) live in `meta/conventions.md`. Follow them.
- **Keep state current.** Check off tasks in `specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md`, append non-obvious
  calls to `decisions.md`, bump `meta/version-record.md` on a refresh.
- **Don't commit or push without the user's go-ahead.** Author changes in the working tree and present
  them for review first — **especially spec/design edits** (`requirements.md`, `design.md`, and the
  `tasks/*` plans): commit only once the user has reviewed and approved that gate. **Never push** (any
  branch, tag, or `main`) without explicit confirmation.
  - **Approval is per-change, not standing.** A go-ahead to commit one slice — or a plan whose steps
    mention committing — does **not** authorize the next commit or push. Don't infer blanket permission
    from a commit-per-slice rhythm. When a slice's work is done, **stop and present it**, then wait for
    an explicit "commit" before committing and an explicit "push" before pushing. When unsure, ask.
- **Author in CommonMark**; no meaning by color/emoji alone (R15).

## Stack

Python throughout — sample codebases (`taskflow-api` FastAPI + SQLModel + pytest; `taskflow-cli`
argparse) and the `tools/` check suite. A project virtualenv lives at `.venv` (pyyaml, jsonschema);
create it with `tools/doctor --setup-venv` (or `make venv`). `make`/`PY` auto-detect `.venv/bin/python`
when present — no manual activation needed.

content.text:
Memory dir is empty. Done. Here's the complete picture.

## What changed

**1. Personal prefs → user-global (`~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, new file)**
The two bucket-B items that span all your projects: small-tool-batches discipline + Read-over-shell. Sync this via dotfiles for cross-machine uniformity.

**2. Project facts → confirmed/tightened in committed `CLAUDE.md`**
Bucket A was already mostly there; I folded the continuity-hygiene specifics (🔓 ledger as canonical deferral home, §3 refresh at phase boundaries) into the "Keep state current" agreement. version-currency, venv, honor-gates, project overview were already covered.

**3. Auto-memory disabled (committed, project scope)**
`.claude/settings.json` → `"autoMemoryEnabled": false`. Verified valid JSON; permissions + hooks untouched. Travels with the repo via git, so it's truly uniform on every machine — *and it's now version-controlled and reviewable*, which was the whole goal.

**4. Project memory dir emptied** — all 8 files removed; nothing lost (every fact lives in a committed or dotfiles-synced file now).

## Two things to flag

**a) Uncommitted changes — your call on when.** Per CLAUDE.md's ask-before-commit/push agreements, I've left these in the working tree for review:
- `.claude/settings.json` (autoMemoryEnabled)
- `CLAUDE.md` (tightened continuity row)

The `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` and the memory-dir deletion are *outside* this repo (not git-tracked), so they're already "live" — no commit needed. Want me to commit the two repo changes? (I'd want an explicit go-ahead, and they're spec-adjacent config, not spec edits.)

**b) The collaborator/teaching caveat you accepted.** Committing `autoMemoryEnabled: false` means anyone who clones this training repo — learners included — gets memory forced off, while **U4 teaches memory as a feature**. That's a real tension. Two ways to soften it if it bothers you later: (i) move the disable to `.claude/settings.local.json` (gitignored, per-machine — but then you'd sync *that* via dotfiles, losing the git-uniformity you chose); or (ii) add a one-line comment/note in the maintainer guide explaining why the course repo itself runs memory-off (dogfooding "source of truth is the files"). I'd lean toward (ii) — it turns the tension into a teaching moment consistent with the repo's thesis. Want me to add that note?

The dotfiles repo for `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is the remaining piece to truly close cross-machine uniformity for your personal layer — that's outside this repo, so I'll leave it unless you want help scaffolding it.

content.text: Let me add the maintainer-guide note. First I'll read the guide to find the right spot.

content.content:
1	# Maintainer guide
2	
3	How to add or update a unit — and refresh the course when the CLI changes — **without breaking the
4	catalog, coverage matrix, or capability map** (R13.AC3). The automated checks are your backstop, but
5	they tell you *that* something broke, not *how to avoid* breaking it; this guide is the "how."
6	
7	If you are picking up this repo cold, start by reading
8	[`../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md) and
9	running the [`prime-context` skill](../.claude/skills/prime-context/SKILL.md) — see the recipe below.
10	
11	## The invariants you must not break
12	
13	Everything cross-cutting lives **once** and is referenced by key (R13.AC2); duplication rots. The
14	[checks](../tools/) enforce these — run `make check` and keep it green:
15	
16	- **Front matter** — every `course/units/NN-slug/unit.md` (generated from `unit.src.md`) validates
17	  against [`unit-frontmatter.schema.json`](../meta/unit-frontmatter.schema.json) (`check-frontmatter`).
18	- **Rendered units** — each unit is **authored** as `unit.src.md` (front matter + `{{vd:<key>}}` version tokens);
19	  the committed, learner-facing `unit.md` is **generated** from it by
20	  [`render-units`](../tools/render-units) (which resolves the tokens), and the unit index
21	  `course/units/README.md` by [`render-index`](../tools/render-index). `make check` fails if either is
22	  out of sync — regenerate with `make render`. **Edit `unit.src.md`, never `unit.md`.**
23	- **Coverage** — every can-do (C1–C17 + CV) is advanced by ≥1 unit, every coverage-matrix area is
24	  covered, and every `advances`/`covered_by` id is real (`check-coverage`).
25	- **Links** — every internal relative link resolves (`check-links`). A link to a not-yet-created file
26	  fails the build, so reference forward targets as plain text until they exist.
27	- **Version references** — every version-data token (the `vd:` references) in `course/**` resolves to
28	  a key in [`version-data.yaml`](../meta/version-data.yaml) (`check-version-refs`).
29	- **Traceability** — every requirement is referenced by a course artifact, and every can-do
30	  traces to ≥1 lab **and** ≥1 capstone-rubric dimension (`check-traceability`). The requirement set is
31	  **discovered** from the `### Rn` headings in `requirements.md`, so adding one needs no edit to the
32	  check (see *Adding a post-v1 enhancement* below).
33	
34	`make check` is the day-to-day guard; `make check-strict` is the release gate (it turns
35	not-yet-authored placeholders into hard failures).
36	
37	## Adding a unit
38	
39	Work in this order — **meta artifacts first, prose second** — so the single sources of truth lead and
40	the checks pass as you go:
41	
42	1. **Decide what it teaches.** If it introduces a genuinely new capability, add the can-do to
43	   [`capability-map.yaml`](../meta/capability-map.yaml) (and regenerate its `.json` twin — never
44	   hand-edit the JSON). Otherwise map the unit to existing can-dos.
45	2. **Register it in the catalog.** Add the unit to
46	   [`use-case-catalog.yaml`](../meta/use-case-catalog.yaml) (`advances:` the can-dos it teaches) and
47	   ensure the [`coverage-matrix.yaml`](../meta/coverage-matrix.yaml) areas it covers point back at it
48	   (`covered_by:`).
49	3. **Create the unit from a template.** Copy [`unit-core.md`](../meta/templates/unit-core.md) (or
50	   [`unit-awareness.md`](../meta/templates/unit-awareness.md) for an awareness-tier unit) to

56	   `solution/uNN-labM` branch, a row in [`../codebases/SEEDED.md`](../codebases/SEEDED.md), and an

59	   as such. The lab must trace a can-do: a `## Lab` heading plus the front-matter `can_do`.

62	   reference it by its version-data key (the token convention is in
63	   [`../meta/conventions.md`](../meta/conventions.md)) (R12.AC3).

65	   [`capstone/rubric.md`](./capstone/rubric.md), or `check-traceability --strict` fails.
66	7. **Sync state and check.** Run the [`close-unit` command](../.claude/commands/close-unit.md) to
67	   update `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3, check the `tasks.md` box, log the decision, and run `make check`.
68	
69	## Updating a unit
70	
71	Edit the prose in `unit.src.md` (never the generated `unit.md`), run `make render`, then keep its
72	references honest: if you rename or move anything it links to, fix the
73	link (`check-links`); if you change what it teaches, update the catalog/coverage entries and the
74	rubric to match (`check-coverage`, `check-traceability`). Re-run `make check`. The in-session hook on
75	edits under `course/`/`meta/` runs the suite for you and blocks on failure.
76	
77	## Authoring a unit *with* Claude Code (the recipe)
78	
79	This is the loop the course was built with, using its own dogfooded extensions (R14.AC2):
80	
81	1. **Prime context.** Run the [`prime-context` skill](../.claude/skills/prime-context/SKILL.md) — it
82	   reads the spec in canonical order, surfaces the live status and open loops, and reports git state,
83	   so the session starts grounded in the files rather than guessing.
84	2. **Work one unit, in a bounded slice.** Load only the relevant requirements, the one `design.md`
85	   section, the front-matter schema, and the cross-cutting `meta/` artifacts the unit references — not
86	   the whole spec. Draft the meta entries, then the unit (as `unit.src.md`, then `make render`) and
87	   lab, against the invariants above.
88	3. **Verify as you go.** Let the edit hook / `make check` catch breakages early; verify any
89	   version-specific claim against the installed CLI, never from memory.
90	4. **Close the unit.** Run [`close-unit`](../.claude/commands/close-unit.md) to sync every state file
91	   and run the checks in one step — the same chore, done the same way each time.
92	
93	## Refreshing when the CLI changes
94	
95	When Claude Code updates, refresh rather than rewrite (R12.AC7). The full process is in the
96	[build case study](./capstone/case-study.md) §5: run
97	[`check-version-drift`](../tools/check-version-drift), re-verify each flagged detail against the
98	installed CLI, update only [`version-data.yaml`](../meta/version-data.yaml), bump
99	[`version-record.md`](../meta/version-record.md), and re-run the suite. Because version-specifics are
100	quarantined, a bump touches a bounded set of files, not the prose.
101	
102	## Adding a post-v1 enhancement (a new requirement)
103	
104	A *refresh* (above) keeps the same scope against a newer CLI. Occasionally the course gains a new
105	**capability of its own** — a new generated artifact, check, or guarantee. Don't bolt it on ad hoc;
106	run it through the same spec-driven, gated path the course teaches and was built with. The exhaustive
107	CLI reference (R16) and the version-change digest (R17) were added exactly this way in **P8** — read
108	[`../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks/P8-cli-reference.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks/P8-cli-reference.md)
109	as the worked example. The additive pattern, each step gated per the root
110	[`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md) working agreements:
111	
112	1. **New requirement.** Add a `### Rn — …` heading with testable EARS acceptance criteria to
113	   [`requirements.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md). **Approve it at the requirements
114	   gate before designing.** IDs are append-only — never renumber existing requirements; downstream
115	   artifacts cite them.
116	2. **Design it.** Amend [`design.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/design.md) with a section that serves
117	   the requirement, each choice tagged with the `[Rn]` it traces to. **Approve at the design gate before
118	   writing tasks.**
119	3. **Plan it.** Add an ordered `tasks/P{N}-<slug>.md` build plan (don't overload an existing phase

121	4. **Record the why.** Append a [`decisions.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md) entry,
122	   update the **🔓 open-loops ledger** for any deferred work, and refresh
123	   [`IMPLEMENTATION.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md) §3 at the phase boundary. The

131

133	design. An enhancement extends the *requirement* set and the artifacts, not the learner-outcome
